name: Tuna
description: Plain, cooked tuna is not toxic to cats and is often used as a flavour enhancer in cat food. However, regular feeding of tuna — especially raw tuna — creates significant health risks: thiamine deficiency, mercury accumulation, and nutritional imbalance. Tuna should be an occasional treat, not a dietary staple.
safetyNotes: Thiaminase risk: raw tuna contains thiaminase, which destroys thiamine (B1); regular raw tuna feeding causes neurological damage — weakness, head tilt, seizures
Mercury risk: tuna is high in mercury; chronic exposure causes neurological damage, kidney damage, and immune suppression
Nutritional imbalance: tuna alone lacks several essential nutrients for cats (calcium, vitamins) and is high in unsaturated fats that deplete vitamin E
Canned tuna in water (plain, no salt): safe as an occasional treat; avoid tuna in oil or with any added salt
A small piece of plain cooked tuna occasionally is fine; daily feeding is not recommended

symptoms.name: While a tiny bite of plain, cooked tuna is generally safe as a rare treat, feeding a cat a diet heavy in tuna
Steatitis (Yellow Fat Disease)
symptoms.onset: Chronic (develops over weeks to months of a diet heavy in tuna).
symptoms.notes: Tuna is extremely high in polyunsaturated fatty acids but naturally lacks adequate Vitamin E2. A heavy tuna diet depletes the cat's Vitamin E reserves, causing the fat tissue to oxidize and become painfully inflamed23. This manifests as firm, exquisitely painful lumps under the skin, making the cat

symptoms.name: Neurological Dysfunction (Methylmercury Poisoning)
symptoms.onset: Chronic (accumulates over time with regular feeding).
symptoms.notes: Because tuna is a large apex predator, it bioaccumulates high levels of methylmercury2. Cats are particularly sensitive to low doses of organic mercury, which easily crosses the blood-brain barrier and interferes with neuronal protein synthesis25. symptoms.name: Neurological Dysfunction (Thiamine Deficiency) (Note
symptoms.onset: Chronic / Delayed (typically develops within 2 to 4 weeks of a raw fish-heavy diet).
symptoms.notes: Raw tuna (such as Skipjack and Yellowfin) can contain thiaminase, an enzyme that destroys thiamine (Vitamin B1)6. symptoms.name: Gastrointestinal Upset and Salt Toxicosis (Note
symptoms.onset: Acute (within hours to a few days of ingestion).
symptoms.notes: Human-grade canned tuna is frequently packed in oil or high-sodium brine12. The high fat content in oil-packed tuna can trigger severe digestive upset or acute pancreatitis910.
